title: Questions

headings.text: Questions
1 - Which suspicious archive did the user download?
URGENT!.zip
2 - Where did the attackers hide the C2 malware file?
C:\Users\Administrator\AppData\Roaming\update.exe
3 - What is the domain of the Command and Control server?
route.m365officesync.workers.dev
4 - How many times did the threat actor fail to log in to the Administrator?
5 - After the successful login, which backdoor user did the attacker create?
support
6 - Which privileged group was the backdoor user added to?
Administrators
7 - Which Windows service was created to persist the Nessie malware?
Data Protection Service
8 - Which scheduled task was created to persist the Troy malware?
AmazonSync
9 - What flag do you get after finding and running the Troy malware?
THM{c2isonschedule!}
10 - What is the parent process image of the “Odin” malware?
c:\windows\explorer.exe
11 - What is the last line that the “Odin” malware outputs?
Done doing bad stuff!
12 - What flag do you get after finding and running the “Kitten” malware?
THM{persistinginbasket!}
13 - What is the biggest threat to most corporate Windows networks?
Ransomware
14 - At which stage is it best to detect and stop the attack (e.g. Exfiltration)?
Initial Access

study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.

workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.
